revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大和岩雄]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1928|2|15}}|death_date={{death date|2021|6|20}}|death_place={{ill|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|1=Empire of Japan}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|historian|qid=Q201788|1=Historian}}|native_name=大和 岩雄|given_name={{ill|Iwao|qid=Q124685413|1=Iwao}}}}
'''Ōwa Iwao''' (大和 岩雄, February 15, 1928 – June 20, 2021) was a Japanese {{ill|Editor|ja|編集者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Editor|1=editing staff|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q876864}}, publishing entrepreneur, and {{ill|Ancient history of Japan|ja|古代史|lt=ancient history|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient history of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} researcher. He was the founder of {{ill|Daiwa Shobō|ja|大和書房|lt=Daiwa Shobō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiwa Shobō|1=Daiwa Shobō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11433473}} and {{ill|Seishun Publishing Company|ja|青春出版社|lt=Seishun Shuppansha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seishun Publishing Company|1=Seishun Publishing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11661550}}.
== Biography and Career == Ōwa was born into a family of {{ill|Gofukushō|ja|呉服商|lt=kimono merchants|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gofukushō|1=kimono dealer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22125847}} dating back to the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} in what is now {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|qid=Q840888|lt=Ina City|1=Ina, Nagano}} (formerly {{ill|Takatō, Nagano|en|Takatō, Nagano|qid=Q7677451|lt=Takato Town|1=Takatō, Nagano}}) in {{ill|Kamiina District, Nagano|en|Kamiina District, Nagano|qid=Q1155054|lt=Kamiina District|1=Kamiina District, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}. Due to the decline of his family’s business, after finishing {{ill|Kōtō shōgakkō (Higher Elementary School)|ja|高等小学校|lt=higher elementary school|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtō shōgakkō (Higher Elementary School)|1=higher elementary school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11672691}}, he planned to become a lathe operator at {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|en|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|qid=Q648280|lt=Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|1=Mitsubishi Heavy Industries}} in {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|qid=Q11751|lt=Nagoya City|1=Nagoya}}. However, in 1942 he entered Nagano Normal School (currently the {{ill|Faculty of Education, Shinshu University|ja|信州大学教育学部|lt=Shinshu University’s Faculty of Education|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Faculty of Education, Shinshu University|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), where he studied at public expense. After graduating in 1948, he was appointed to teach {{ill|National language|en|National language|qid=Q319972|lt=Japanese language|1=National language}}, and {{ill|Music|en|Music|qid=Q638|lt=music|1=Music}} at Kashio Middle School in {{ill|Ōshika, Nagano|en|Ōshika, Nagano|qid=Q1203751|lt=Ōshika Village|1=Ōshika, Nagano}}, {{ill|Shimoina District, Nagano|en|Shimoina District, Nagano|qid=Q726999|lt=Shimoina District|1=Shimoina District, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture. He resigned in December 1948 and moved to {{ill|Nagano (city)|en|Nagano (city)|qid=Q128849|lt=Nagano City|1=Nagano (city)}}, where he joined the magazine {{ill|Ashi (magazine)|ja|葦|lt=Ashi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashi (magazine)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヨシ|1=Phragmites australis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28557}}, edited by {{ill|Yamamoto Shigemitsu|ja|山本茂実|lt=Yamamoto Shigemitsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamoto Shigemitsu|1=Shigemi Yamamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11469341}}. Beginning with issue No.4, he served as editor-in-chief of Ashi. Among the sales staff was {{ill|Ozawa Kazuichi|ja|小澤和一|lt=Ozawa Kazuichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ozawa Kazuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
With Ashi’s success, he moved to Tokyo in 1950. While still editing Ashi, he started “{{ill|Jinsei Techō|ja|人生手帖|lt=Jinsei Techō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinsei Techō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” in 1951 with a print run of 1,000 copies in cooperation with {{ill|Bunri Shoin|ja|文理書院|lt=Bunri Shoin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunri Shoin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, Ashi became increasingly politicized, leading him to depart from the magazine. By 1955, Jinsei Techō reached a circulation of 70,000. This rapid growth attracted criticism, and the {{ill|Yomiuri Shimbun|en|Yomiuri Shimbun|qid=Q645218|lt=Yomiuri Shimbun|1=The Yomiuri Shimbun}} social affairs department falsely reported it as a secret organ of the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|en|Japanese Communist Party|qid=Q641600|lt=Japanese Communist Party|1=Japanese Communist Party}} for infiltration of the {{ill|Japan Self-Defense Forces|en|Japan Self-Defense Forces|qid=Q275488|lt=JSDF|1=Japan Self-Defense Forces}}. Ōwa refuted this in Jinsei Techō. In 1956, he resigned as editor-in-chief, and in May of the same year, together with Ozawa Kazuichi, he founded {{ill|Seishun Publishing Company|ja|青春出版社|lt=Seishun Shuppansha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seishun Publishing Company|1=Seishun Publishing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11661550}}, achieving success with practical books.
In 1960, Ōwa transferred management rights of the company to Ozawa and, together with the newly started magazine "Seishun no Techō," established another publishing house. In July 1963, he renamed it {{ill|Daiwa Shobō|ja|大和書房|lt=Daiwa Shobō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiwa Shobō|1=Daiwa Shobō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11433473}}. In 1964, “{{ill|Ai to Shi o Mitsumete|ja|愛と死をみつめて|lt=Ai to Shi o Mitsumete|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ai to Shi o Mitsumete|1=Ai to Shi o Mitsumete|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11493207}},” a collection of letters exchanged between a terminally ill female college student and her lover, became a bestseller, exceeding 1.3 million copies.<ref name="jiji20210625" /><ref name="nikkei20210625"/>
On February 4, 1966, Ōwa visited the {{ill|Sapporo Snow Festival|en|Sapporo Snow Festival|qid=Q929531|lt=Sapporo Snow Festival|1=Sapporo Snow Festival}} and had planned to return by plane. However, he decided to meet someone arriving from Tokyo the next day and got off the airport-bound bus halfway. The All Nippon Airways flight he would have taken crashed in Tokyo Bay off {{ill|Haneda Airport|en|Haneda Airport|qid=Q204853|lt=Haneda|1=Haneda Airport}} (the {{ill|All Nippon Airways Flight 60|en|All Nippon Airways Flight 60|qid=Q4729189|lt=accident|1=All Nippon Airways Flight 60}}). Newspapers erroneously reported his death, and he received numerous condolence telegrams. On November 14, 1966, after the death of his mother, he retired from editing at the age of 38 and dedicated himself solely to management.
In 1973, he established {{ill|Daiwa Shuppan|ja|大和出版|lt=Daiwa Shuppan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiwa Shuppan|1=Daiwa Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17225604}} as a related company.
In 1974, at the suggestion of {{ill|Suzuki Takeki|ja|鈴木武樹|lt=Suzuki Takeki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzuki Takeki|1=Takeju Suzuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11648993}}, he founded the quarterly historical magazine “{{ill|Tōajia no Kodai Bunka (East Asia’s Ancient Culture)|ja|東アジアの古代文化|lt=East Asia no Kodai Bunka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōajia no Kodai Bunka (East Asia’s Ancient Culture)|1=Ancient Cultures of East Asia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11523812}}.” Suzuki stepped down from editing after the fourth issue due to other commitments, and from issue five until the final issue (No.137) in January 2005, Ōwa served as editor.<ref>{{Cite|和書|last=大和|first=岩雄|title=編集後記|journal=東アジアの古代文化|issue=50|publisher=大和書房|year=1987|month=1|page=360}}</ref><ref>{{Cite|和書|last=大和|first=岩雄|title=［編集後記］最終刊にあたって|journal=東アジアの古代文化|issue=137|publisher=大和書房|year=2009|month=1|pages=473-475}}</ref> He himself took an interest in ancient history and authored numerous works in the field, publishing them through his own company.
In 1988, he stepped down as president of Daiwa Shobō and became chairman.
On June 20, 2021, at 5:15 AM, Ōwa died of {{ill|Heart failure|en|Heart failure|qid=Q181754|lt=heart failure|1=Heart failure}} at a hospital in Tokyo.<ref name="jiji20210625">{{Cite news|title=大和岩雄氏死去 大和書房創業者、古代史研究家|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210625040326/https://www.jiji.com/jc/article?k=2021062500518&g=soc|agency=時事通信社|publisher=時事ドットコム|date=2021-06-25|accessdate=2021-06-25}}</ref><ref name="nikkei20210625">{{Cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXZQOUE253ER0V20C21A6000000/|title=大和岩雄氏が死去 大和書房創業者|newspaper=日本経済新聞社|agency=共同通信社|date=2021-06-25|accessdate=2021-06-25}}</ref> He was {{age at death|1928|2|15|2021|6|20}} years old.
== Works == * ’Nihon kodai shiron’ (日本古代試論), Daiwa Shobō, 1974 * ’Kojiki seiritsukō: Nihon saiko no koten e no gimon’ (古事記成立考 日本最古の古典への疑問), Daiwa Shobō, 1975 * ’Kojiki gishosetsu no shūhen’ (古事記偽書説の周辺), Meicho Shuppan, 1979 * ’Kojiki to Tenmu Tennō no nazo’ (古事記とEmperor Tenmuの謎), Rokkō Shuppan, 1979 * ’Nihon kodai ōken shiron: Kodai Kankoku to no kankei o chūshin ni’ (日本古代王権試論 古代韓国との関連を中心に), Meicho Shuppan, 1981 * ’Amaterasu Ōmikami to zempō kōenfun no nazo’ (天照大神と前方後円墳の謎), Rokkō Shuppan, 1983 * ’"Nihon" koku wa itsu dekitaka: Nihon kokugō no tanjō’ ("日本"国はいつできたか 日本国号の誕生), Rokkō Shuppan, 1985 * ’Tenmu Tennō shusshō no nazo’ (Emperor Tenmu出生の謎), Rokkō Shuppan, 1987 * ’Tenmu Tennō ron’ (Emperor Tenmu論), 2 vols., Daiwa Shobō, 1987 * ’"Kojiki" gishosetsu wa naritanai ka’ (『古事記』偽書説は成り立たないか), Daiwa Shobō, 1988 * ’Hito ni sukareru chie 97 shō: aisareru josei ni naru tame no hiketshu-shū’ (人に好かれる知恵97章 愛される女性になるための秘訣集), Daiwa Shobō, 1988 * ’Jinja to kodai ōken saishi’ (神社と古代王権祭祀), Hakusuisha, 1989 * ’Jinja to kodai minkan saishi’ (神社と古代民間祭祀), Hakusuisha, 1989 * ’Yamataikoku wa nikasho atta: Yamataikoku kara shoki Yamato ōken e’ (邪馬台国は二カ所あった 邪馬台国から初期ヤマト王権へ), Daiwa Shobō, 1990 * ’Hitomaro no jitsuzō’ (柿本人麻呂の実像), Daiwa Shobō, 1990 * ’Shinano kodai shikō’ (信濃古代史考), Meicho Shuppan, 1990 * ’Tenchi・Tenmu Tennō no nazo: Nihon shoki no kyogi to shinjitsu’ (天智・Emperor Tenmuの謎 『日本書紀』の虚偽と真実), Rokkō Shuppan, 1991 * ’Hitomaro densetsu’ (人麻呂伝説), Hakusuisha, 1991 * ’Oni to tennō’ (鬼と天皇), Hakusuisha, 1992 * ’Yūjo to tennō’ (遊女と天皇), Hakusuisha, 1993 * ’Hata-shi no kenkyū: Nihon no bunka to shinkō ni fukaku kan’yoshita torai shūdan no kenkyū’ (秦氏の研究 日本の文化と信仰に深く関与した渡来集団の研究), Daiwa Shobō, 1993 * ’Nihon ni atta Chōsen ōkoku: nazo no "Shin ōkoku" to kodai shinkō’ (日本にあった朝鮮王国 謎の「秦王国」と古代信仰), Hakusuisha, 1993 * ’Jūjika to uzumaki: shōchō to shite no sei to shi’ (十字架と渦巻 象徴としての生と死), Hakusuisha, 1995 * ’Majo wa naze sora o tobu ka’ (魔女はなぜ空を飛ぶか), Daiwa Shobō, 1995 * ’Majo wa naze hito o kū ka’ (魔女はなぜ人を喰うか), Daiwa Shobō, 1996 ** Combined edition: ’Majo-ron: naze sora o tobi, hito o kū ka’ (魔女論 なぜ空を飛び、人を喰うか), Daiwa Shobō, 2011 * ’Tengu to tennō’ (天狗と天皇), Hakusuisha, 1997 * ’Kamigami no kōkogaku’ (神々の考古学), Daiwa Shobō, 1998 * ’Shin Yamataikokuron: Jo’ō no miyako wa nikasho atta’ (新邪馬台国論 女王の都は二ヵ所あった), Daiwa Shobō, 2000 * ’Hashihaka wa Himiko no haka ka’ (箸墓は卑弥呼の墓か), Daiwa Shobō, 2004 * ’Nihon shoki seiritsukō: Tenmu・Tenchi ifu-keitei kō’ (日本書紀成立考 天武・天智異父兄弟考), Daiwa Shobō, 2010 * ’Kami to hito no kodaigaku: taiyō shinkōron’ (神と人の古代学 太陽信仰論), Daiwa Shobō, 2012 * ’Zoku・Hata-shi no kenkyū (Nihon no sangyō to shinkō ni fukaku kan’yoshita torai shūdan no kenkyū)’ (続・秦氏の研究(日本の産業と信仰に深く関与した渡来集団の研究)), Daiwa Shobō, 2013 * ’"Kojiki" seiritsu no nazo o saguru’ (『古事記』成立の謎を探る), Daiwa Shobō, 2013 * ’Nihon shinwa ron’ (日本神話論), Daiwa Shobō, 2015
=== Co-authored and Edited Works === * ’Fujinoki kofun to rokuseiki: hisōsha wa dare ka’ (藤ノ木古墳と六世紀 被葬者は誰か), co-authored with {{ill|Kuroiwa Jūgo|ja|黒岩重吾|lt=Kuroiwa Jūgo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroiwa Jūgo|1=Jūgo Kuroiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11678140}}, Daiwa Shobō, 1989 * ’Himiko to Yamataikoku’ (卑弥呼と邪馬台国), co-authored with Kuroiwa Jūgo, Daiwa Shobō, 1992 * ’Yamataikoku no jidai’ (邪馬台国の時代), co-authored with Kuroiwa Jūgo, Daiwa Shobō, 1997 * ’Minshū-shi no isan’ (民衆史の遺産), 10 vols., co-chief editor with {{ill|Tanigawa Ken’ichi|ja|谷川健一|lt=Tanigawa Ken’ichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken’ichi|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2392596}}, Daiwa Shobō, 2012–16
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== Sources == * “Gendai Nihon Jinmei-roku 2002” (Contemporary Japanese Biographical Dictionary 2002), Nichigai Associates
{{translated page|ja|大和岩雄|version=103673255|comment=Imported full ja history}}
